---
title: "BikeTrips_SeasonChange"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---


## GAMM to Figure out Peak Season Times
```{r}
model_data_2025 <- readRDS(here("07_ExplanatoryModelling", "model_data_2025.rds"))

library(lubridate)

model_data_2025 <- model_data_2025 %>%
  mutate(day_of_year = yday(date)) |>
  arrange(date) %>%
  mutate(dow = factor(dow, ordered = FALSE)) |>
  mutate(
    # How many people biked yesterday?
    lag_total_trips = lag(total_trips, n = 1),
    dow_mon = fct_relevel(dow, "Mon")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    day_category = case_when(
      is_holiday == 1 ~ "Holiday",       # 1. Pulls out ALL holidays first
      dow == "Sat" ~ "Saturday",    # 2. Labels remaining Saturdays
      dow == "Sun" ~ "Sunday",        # 3. Labels remaining Sundays
      TRUE ~ "Weekday"                   # 4. Everything else becomes the baseline
    ),
    # Set "Weekday" as the baseline (0)
    day_category = factor(day_category, levels = c("Weekday", "Saturday", "Sunday", "Holiday"))
  ) |>
  mutate(
    day_type1 = ifelse(is_holiday, "Holiday", as.character(dow)),
    day_type1 = fct_relevel(day_type1, "Mon") # Keep Monday as baseline
  ) |>
  mutate(is_holiday = as.factor(is_holiday))
```



```{r}
fit_gam <- gam(
  total_trips ~ s(temp_max) + 
                s(day_of_year, k = 5) + # The seasonal 'activation' engine
                precip_hour_count +
                dow_mon,
  family = nb(), 
  data = model_data_2025,
  method = "REML"
)
```

```{r}
# 3. View the business coefficients
summary(fit_gam)

# 4. Check the ACF 
acf(residuals(fit_gam, type = "pearson"), na.action = na.pass, main = "ACF: Dynamic GAM (Lagged Response)")
```




```{r}
library(gratia)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork) # For combining plots

# 1. Generate the standard nice plots
# 'grouped = TRUE' puts them in a nice grid
p_all <- draw(fit_gam, residuals = TRUE) 

# 2. Customize with your BIA Branding
# We can't edit draw() directly easily, so we build them for ultimate control:
sm_temp <- smooth_estimates(fit_gam, smooth = "s(temp_max)")
sm_day  <- smooth_estimates(fit_gam, smooth = "s(day_of_year)")


# Plot B: Seasonality (The k=5 Engine)
g2 <- ggplot(sm_day, aes(x = day_of_year, y = .estimate)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = .estimate - 2*.se, ymax = .estimate + 2*.se), fill = "#1A5276", alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_line(color = "#D4AC0D", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Impact on Bike Ridership Volume",
    subtitle = "Partial effect of the day of year on total ridership volume",
    x = "Day of Year", 
    y = "Partial Effect (Log Scale)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

g2 # Display side-by-side
```



### Activation Dates






```{r}
library(gratia)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Plot the Seasonal Smooth (The "Hill")
p1 <- draw(fit_gam, select = "s(day_of_year)") +
  labs(title = "Seasonal Trend: Biker Volume")

# 2. Plot the Derivative (The "Acceleration")
# This shows where the neighborhood is speeding up or slowing down
d <- derivatives(fit_gam, select = "s(day_of_year)", n = 300)
p2 <- draw(d) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Rate of Change (The Pulse)")

# Combine them (if you have patchwork) or plot separately
p1
p2
```


```{r}
library(gratia)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)


# 2. Calculate Velocity (f')
d1 <- derivatives(fit_gam, select = "s(day_of_year)", order = 1, n = 303)


# B. Max Momentum (Inflection Point): Peak of the 1st derivative
day_inflection <- d1 %>% filter(day_of_year < 200) %>% slice_max(.derivative, n = 1) %>% pull(day_of_year)

# C. Season Peak (The Summit): When velocity hits zero
day_peak <- d1 %>% filter(day_of_year > day_inflection) %>% filter(abs(.derivative) == min(abs(.derivative))) %>% pull(day_of_year)

# D. Deactivation (Max Decline): When velocity is most negative
day_decline <- d1 %>% filter(day_of_year > day_peak) %>% slice_min(.derivative, n = 1) %>% pull(day_of_year)

# 4. Create the Results Table
key_dates <- data.frame(
  Event = c("Season Start", "Peak Season", "Rapid Decline Starts"),
  Day_of_Year = c(day_inflection, day_peak, day_decline)
) %>%
  mutate(Date = as.Date(Day_of_Year, origin = "2025-01-01"))

print(key_dates)
```


```{r}
# Function to get the typical temp range for a specific day
get_temp_range <- function(target_day, data) {
  data %>%
    filter(day_of_year >= (target_day - 3) & day_of_year <= (target_day + 3)) %>%
    summarise(
      min_temp = round(quantile(temp_max, 0.25, na.rm=TRUE), 1),
      max_temp = round(quantile(temp_max, 0.75, na.rm=TRUE), 1)
    ) %>%
    mutate(Range = paste0(min_temp, "°C to ", max_temp, "°C")) %>%
    pull(Range)
}

# Apply to your dates
key_dates$Typical_Temp_Max <- sapply(key_dates$Day_of_Year, get_temp_range, data = model_data_2025)

print(key_dates[, c("Event", "Date", "Typical_Temp_Max")])
```



```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(gratia)

# 1. Generate a high-resolution version of the derivatives
# Using more points (n = 1000) helps pinpoint the exact peak
d1_high_res <- derivatives(fit_gam, select = "s(day_of_year)", n = 1000)

# 2. Find the row where the derivative is at its absolute minimum
steepest_row <- d1_high_res %>%
  slice(which.min(.derivative))

# 3. Extract the numeric day and convert to a date
steepest_day <- steepest_row$day_of_year
steepest_date <- as.Date(steepest_day - 1, origin = "2025-01-01")

# 4. Print the result
print(paste("The steepest decline occurs on Day:", round(steepest_day, 1)))
print(paste("Calendar Date:", steepest_date))
```





##



```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(gratia)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Calculate High-Res Velocity (f')
# n=1000 provides sub-day precision for pinpointing peaks
d1_hires <- derivatives(fit_gam, select = "s(day_of_year)", order = 1, n = 303)

# 2. Extract Key Milestones
# A. Max Momentum (Spring Surge): Steepest positive slope
day_inflection <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year < 200) %>% 
  slice_max(.derivative, n = 1) %>% 
  pull(day_of_year)

# B. The Summit (Peak Volume): Where velocity crosses zero
day_peak <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year > day_inflection) %>% 
  filter(abs(.derivative) == min(abs(.derivative))) %>% 
  pull(day_of_year)

# C. Steepest Decline (Shoulder Core): Most negative velocity
day_decline <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year > day_peak) %>% 
  slice_min(.derivative, n = 1) %>% 
  pull(day_of_year)

# 3. Create the Professional Results Table
# Using 2025 as the reference year for dates
key_dates <- data.frame(
  Event = c("Spring Momentum Peak", "Season Summit (Max Volume)", "Core Shoulder Decline"),
  Day_of_Year = round(c(day_inflection, day_peak, day_decline), 1),
  Calendar_Date = as.Date(c(day_inflection, day_peak, day_decline) - 1, origin = "2025-01-01")
)

print(key_dates)

# 4. The "Momentum" Plot
draw(d1_hires) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(day_inflection, day_peak, day_decline), 
             linetype = "dashed", color = c("#27ae60", "#2980b9", "#c0392b"), size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = day_inflection, y = 0.015, label = "Spring Surge", color = "#27ae60", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = day_peak, y = 0.015, label = "The Summit", color = "#2980b9", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = day_decline, y = -0.005, label = "Steepest Decline", color = "#c0392b", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "Beach BIA Ridership Momentum (f')",
       subtitle = "High-resolution derivative analysis of seasonal shifts",
       y = "Velocity (Trips per Day/Day)",
       x = "Day of Year") +
  theme_minimal()
```



```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(gratia)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. High-resolution derivatives (n=1000 for sub-day precision)
d1_hires <- derivatives(fit_gam, select = "s(day_of_year)", n = 1000)

# 2. Extract the Four Key Milestones
day_awakening <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year < 125) %>% 
  slice(which.min(abs(.derivative))) %>% pull(day_of_year)

day_inflection <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year < 200) %>% 
  slice_max(.derivative, n = 1) %>% pull(day_of_year)

day_summit <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year > 150) %>% 
  slice(which.min(abs(.derivative))) %>% pull(day_of_year)

day_decline <- d1_hires %>% 
  filter(day_of_year > day_summit) %>% 
  slice_min(.derivative, n = 1) %>% pull(day_of_year)

# 3. Create the Combined Professional Results Table
master_calendar <- data.frame(
  Milestone = c("Spring Awakening", "Spring Surge", "Summer Summit", "Steepest Decline"),
  Role = c("Rock Bottom", "Max Growth Rate", "Max Volume", "Shoulder Core"),
  Day = round(c(day_awakening, day_inflection, day_summit, day_decline), 0),
  Date = as.Date(round(c(day_awakening, day_inflection, day_summit, day_decline), 0) - 1, origin = "2025-01-01")
)
print(master_calendar)

# 4. Master Plot with Edits
draw(d1_hires) +
  # --- EDIT: Force the iconic blue onto the derivative line ---
  geom_line(aes(y = .derivative), color = "#1A5276", linewidth = 1.1) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "black", alpha = 0.3) +
  # --- EDIT: Reduced linewidth from 1 to 0.5 for lighter dashes ---
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(day_awakening, day_inflection, day_summit, day_decline), 
             linetype = "dashed", color = c("#4477AA", "#27ae60", "#EE6677", "#E69F00"), linewidth = 0.5) + 
  annotate("text", x = day_awakening, y = 0.005, label = "Awakening", 
           color = "#4477AA", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5, fontface ="bold") +
  annotate("text", x = day_inflection, y = 0.005, label = "Spring Surge", 
           color = "#27ae60", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5, fontface ="bold") +
  annotate("text", x = day_summit, y = 0.005, label = "The Summit", 
           color = "#EE6677", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5, fontface ="bold") +
  annotate("text", x = day_decline, y = -0.002, label = "Steepest Decline", 
           color = "#E69F00", angle = 90, vjust = -0.5, fontface ="bold") +
  labs(title = "The Full Seasonal Lifecycle of Beach BIA Ridership",
       subtitle = "Calculated using First Derivative of k = 5 spline, shown below",
       y = "Velocity (Growth/Decline Rate)",
       x = "Day of Year") +
  theme_minimal()
```





```{r}
library(dplyr)

# 1. Define the weather look-up function
get_milestone_weather <- function(target_day, weather_data) {
  weather_data %>%
    # Look at the week surrounding the milestone (target day +/- 3 days)
    filter(day_of_year >= (target_day - 3) & day_of_year <= (target_day + 3)) %>%
    summarise(
      avg_high = mean(temp_max, na.rm = TRUE),
      lower_iqr = quantile(temp_max, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      upper_iqr = quantile(temp_max, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
}

# 2. Apply it to your master_calendar table
# We use rowwise() to ensure the function runs for each specific day
final_bia_report <- master_calendar %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    # Pull the weather stats
    weather_stats = list(get_milestone_weather(Day, model_data_2025)),
    # Format the results into clean strings for your report
    Typical_High = round(weather_stats$avg_high, 1),
    Temp_Range   = paste0(round(weather_stats$lower_iqr, 1), "°C to ", 
                          round(weather_stats$upper_iqr, 1), "°C")
  ) %>%
  select(-weather_stats) # Remove the list-column to keep the table clean

print(final_bia_report)
```


```{r}
# 1. The Function (Updated to be explicit about the column)
get_milestone_weather <- function(target_day, weather_data) {
  weather_data %>%
    # Using .data$ helps R find the column inside the dataframe specifically
    filter(.data$day_of_year >= (target_day - 3) & .data$day_of_year <= (target_day + 3)) %>%
    summarise(
      avg_high = mean(temp_max, na.rm = TRUE),
      lower_iqr = quantile(temp_max, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      upper_iqr = quantile(temp_max, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop" # Keeps the output clean
    )
}

# 2. The Mutate Block
final_bia_report <- master_calendar %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    # Pull the weather stats
    weather_stats = list(get_milestone_weather(Day, model_data_2025)),
    # Extract from the list-column
    Typical_High = round(weather_stats$avg_high, 1),
    Temp_Range   = paste0(round(weather_stats$lower_iqr, 1), "°C to ", 
                          round(weather_stats$upper_iqr, 1), "°C")
  ) %>%
  select(-weather_stats)

print(final_bia_report)

colnames(model_data_2025)
```
